overview.body: Work through each Gaddis program in order. Chapter 7 introduces lists — the most important data structure in the course. Type every program by hand. Lists unlock a whole new level of problem-solving, and the patterns here (append, iterate, accumulate, search, modify) show up in every Lab assignment from here on.
objectives: Create a list with a fixed size using the repetition operator
Build a list dynamically using append() in a loop
Search a list using the in operator
Find an item's index using index() and handle ValueError with try-except
Add items at a specific position using insert()
Remove items using remove() and handle ValueError with try-except
Calculate a list average using both a manual accumulator and sum()
Combine functions that build, process, and return lists
Write a list to a file by looping and converting each item to a string
Read a file into a list using readlines() and convert each element
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 7 covers list creation, indexing, the in operator, append(), insert(), remove(), index(), sum(), len(), min(), max(), passing lists to functions, returning lists from functions, and file I/O with lists.
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 7: Lists and Tuples
programRequirements: Each program is saved as its own individual .py file
Each file uses the exact filename listed in the instructions
Each program runs without syntax errors and output matches the textbook
overview.filenameNote: This chapter uses 10 individual files. Submit all 10 files together in your final CodeGrade submission. Note that read_number_list.py reads a file created by write_number_list.py — run Checkpoint 9 before Checkpoint 10.

rows.task: Create a fixed-size list and fill it with user input
Search a list with the in operator
Build a list dynamically with append()
Find and replace an item using index()
Add an item at a specific position with insert()
Calculate an average with accumulator and sum()
Combine functions that build and process a list
Write a list to a file
Read a file into a list with readlines()

content.body: A program that creates a five-element list pre-filled with zeros using the repetition operator ([0] * NUM_DAYS), then uses a for loop with range(len(sales)) to collect a sales value for each index position. A second for loop displays all the values. This is the standard pattern for working with a list of known size.

items: Open your textbook to Program 7-1.
Create a new file named sales_list.py.
Type the program as shown, including the NUM_DAYS constant.
Run it and enter five sales values.
Confirm all five values are displayed back correctly.

content.title: [0] * 5 Creates a List of Five Zeros
content.body: The expression [0] * NUM_DAYS creates a list with NUM_DAYS elements, each initialized to 0. This reserves the space before you fill it. The for loop then replaces each 0 with user input using the index: sales[index] = float(input(...)).

A product lookup program that stores a list of product numbers and uses the in operator to check whether a user's search value is in the list. If found, a found message appears; if not, a not-found message appears. Short program, but the in operator is one of the most useful tools in Python.

items: Open your textbook to Program 7-2.
Create a new file named in_list.py.
Type the program as shown.
Run it and search for V475 — confirm the found message appears.
Run it again and search for X999 — confirm the not-found message appears.

content.title: in Works with Any List
content.body: if search in prod_nums evaluates to True if search is anywhere in the list, False otherwise. This works with lists of strings, integers, or any type. It is much cleaner than looping through the list yourself to search for a match.

A program that starts with an empty list and builds it dynamically by appending user-entered names inside a sentinel loop. After the loop ends, a for loop displays every name in the list. This is the most common way to build a list when you don't know in advance how many items there will be.

items: Open your textbook to Program 7-3.
Create a new file named list_append.py.
Type the program as shown.
Run it and add three or four names, then stop.
Confirm all names are displayed in the order you entered them.

content.title: append() Always Adds to the End
content.body: Each call to name_list.append(name) adds the new name after all existing items. The list grows by one element each time. Starting with an empty list [] and building with append() is the pattern you will use in almost every Lab that collects a variable number of items.

A program that lets the user change an item in a list by searching for it with index(), which returns the item's position. The program then gets a replacement value and overwrites the old item at that position. A try-except catches ValueError if the item isn't found.

items: Open your textbook to Program 7-4.
Create a new file named index_list.py.
Type the program as shown including the try-except block.
Run it and change 'Burgers' to 'Tacos' — confirm the list updates.
Run it again and try to change 'Sushi' (not in the list) — confirm the error message appears.

content.title: index() Raises ValueError If the Item Is Not Found
content.body: If the item you're searching for isn't in the list, index() raises ValueError — which crashes your program if unhandled. The try-except block in this program catches that and prints a friendly message instead. Always protect index() calls with try-except or an in check first.

A program that creates a list with three names, displays it, then inserts 'Joe' at position 0 (the very beginning) using insert(0, 'Joe'). All existing items shift one position to the right. The list is displayed again to show the result.

items: Open your textbook to Program 7-5.
Create a new file named insert_list.py.
Type the program as shown.
Run it and compare the before and after output.
Try changing insert(0, 'Joe') to insert(1, 'Joe') and see where 'Joe' ends up.

content.title: insert(index, item) Shifts Everything Right
content.body: insert(0, 'Joe') puts 'Joe' at the front and shifts 'James', 'Kathryn', and 'Bill' to positions 1, 2, and 3. insert(1, 'Joe') would put 'Joe' between 'James' and 'Kathryn'. Unlike append(), insert() can place an item anywhere.

A program that lets the user remove an item from a food list by value. remove() finds the first matching item and deletes it, shortening the list by one. A try-except block handles the case where the item doesn't exist.

items: Open your textbook to Program 7-6.
Create a new file named remove_item.py.
Type the program as shown including the try-except block.
Run it and remove 'Chips' — confirm the list shortens.
Run it again and try to remove 'Sushi' — confirm the error message appears.

content.title: remove() Raises ValueError If the Item Is Not Found
content.body: Just like index(), remove() raises ValueError if the item isn't in the list. The try-except block handles this gracefully. If you need to check first without raising an error, use if item in food: before calling remove().

A program that calculates the average of a list of scores two different ways: first with a manual accumulator loop (total1), and then with the built-in sum() function (total2). Both produce the same result. This demonstrates that sum() is a clean shortcut for the accumulator pattern you already know.

items: Open your textbook to Program 7-9.
Create a new file named average_list.py.
Type the program as shown, including both calculation approaches.
Run it and confirm both averages are identical.

content.title: sum(), min(), max(), and len() Work on Any Numeric List
content.body: Python's built-in list functions are powerful shortcuts. sum(scores) replaces a for loop accumulator. len(scores) gives the count. min(scores) and max(scores) find the extremes. You will use all four of these in upcoming Lab assignments.

A grade calculator spread across three functions: get_scores() builds a list of test scores using append() and returns it, get_total() accepts a list and returns the sum using an accumulator, and main() ties them together — calling min() to find the lowest score, dropping it from the total, and calculating the adjusted average. This is the most complex program in Chapter 7 and directly mirrors Lab assignment structure.

items: Open your textbook to Program 7-12.
Create a new file named drop_lowest_score.py.
Type all three functions and the if __name__ == '__main__': guard.
Run it and enter at least four test scores.
Confirm the displayed average excludes the lowest score you entered.

content.title: Functions Can Build and Return Lists
content.body: get_scores() creates an empty list, appends values inside a loop, and returns the finished list. main() stores that return value in scores. This is exactly the pattern used in Lab assignments where a helper function collects data and main() processes it. Lists are passed to and returned from functions just like any other value.

A program that creates a list of seven integers and writes them to numberlist.txt by looping through the list and calling write() on each item converted to a string with str(). Checkpoint 10 reads this file back into a list.

items: Open your textbook to Program 7-16.
Create a new file named write_number_list.py.
Type the program as shown.
Run it — no output appears, but numberlist.txt is created.
Open numberlist.txt to confirm the seven numbers are each on their own line.

content.title: Loop Through the List and Convert Each Item
content.body: for item in numbers: outfile.write(str(item) + '\n') is the standard pattern for writing a list to a file. Each element is converted to a string with str(), then written with a newline. Run this before Checkpoint 10 or read_number_list.py will fail.

A program that reads numberlist.txt back into a list using readlines(), which returns every line as a string in a list. A for loop with range(len(numbers)) then converts each string element to an integer in place by assigning back to numbers[index]. The finished list is printed.

items: Open your textbook to Program 7-17.
Create a new file named read_number_list.py.
Type the program as shown.
Make sure write_number_list.py has been run first to create numberlist.txt.
Run it and confirm the list of integers prints correctly.

content.title: readlines() Returns a List of Strings
content.body: infile.readlines() reads the entire file and returns a list where each element is one line as a string, including the trailing \n. The conversion loop int(numbers[index]) strips the \n automatically when it parses the string — the same behavior as int(readline()) from Chapter 6.

beforeYouSubmit: sales_list.py runs and output matches the book
in_list.py runs and output matches the book
list_append.py runs and output matches the book
index_list.py runs and output matches the book
insert_list.py runs and output matches the book
remove_item.py runs and output matches the book
average_list.py runs and output matches the book
drop_lowest_score.py runs and output matches the book
read_number_list.py runs and displays the list of integers correctly
finalChecklist.submissionReminder: CodeGrade only keeps your most recent submission. Your final upload must include all 10 files or you will lose credit for any that are missing.
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: These are Chapter 7 BookEx exercises. You are typing Gaddis programs exactly as written. All programs use the def main() and if __name__ == '__main__': structure from Chapter 5.

specific: List creation: [], [0] * n, and literal lists
Iteration: for item in list and for index in range(len(list))
The in operator for membership testing
sum(), len(), min(), max() built-in functions
Passing lists to functions and returning lists from functions
Writing lists to files and reading files into lists with readlines()
try-except with ValueError for index() and remove()
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: list comprehensions, classes, anything from chapters not yet covered

ifYouGetStuck: If you get ValueError from index() or remove(), the item isn't in the list — check the try-except block and make sure it matches the book
If read_number_list.py fails with FileNotFoundError, run write_number_list.py first to create numberlist.txt
If your list output looks wrong, check whether you're iterating by value (for item in list) or by index (for index in range(len(list)))
Submit to CodeGrade early — it shows exactly which files pass and which need work
Post in the course Discord if you are stuck after two attempts (describe the error, do not post your code)
Come to Zoom or office hours if you are still stuck
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 7 BookEx Reference Guide — step-by-step breakdown of every program in this chapter
